subUnits.title: 8.1 Calculations Involving Mass & Volume (Stoichiometry)
notes: Stoichiometry: Quantitative relationship between reactants and products in a chemical reaction.
1. Write and BALANCE the chemical equation
3. Use mole ratio from balanced equation
4. Convert moles of required substance to mass/volume
• Gas Volume ↔ Moles (STP): V = n × 22.4 dm³
1. Mass-Mass: Given mass A → find mass B
Example: Mass of CaO from 50g CaCO₃
2. Mass-Volume: Given mass → find gas volume at STP
Example: Volume of H₂ from 6.5g Zn + excess HCl
65g Zn → 1 mol H₂ (22.4 dm³), so 6.5g → 2.24 dm³
3. Volume-Volume: Gas reactants/products at same T & P
Gay-Lussac's Law: Volumes react in simple whole number ratios
10 dm³ N₂ reacts with 30 dm³ H₂ to give 20 dm³ NH₃
• Reactant completely consumed first, determines max product yield
• Always < 100% due to side reactions, incomplete reactions, losses
diagramDescriptions: Stoichiometry calculation flowchart: given → moles → mole ratio → moles → required
Mass-mass, mass-volume, volume-volume comparison table with examples
Limiting reactant analogy diagram (e.g., cars needing 4 tires each, limited by tires not engines)
Percentage yield calculation example with actual vs theoretical bars
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Stoichiometry mass volume calculations chemistry WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The first step in stoichiometric calculations is to:
opts: Write and balance the chemical equation
quiz.explanation: Balanced equation provides the mole ratio needed for all subsequent calculations.

quiz.q: In Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂, 65g Zn produces what volume of H₂ at STP?
quiz.explanation: 65g Zn = 1 mol. Mole ratio Zn:H₂ = 1:1, so 1 mol H₂ = 22.4 dm³ at STP.

quiz.q: Gay-Lussac's Law states that gases react in:
quiz.explanation: At constant T & P, gas volumes react and form in simple whole number ratios.

quiz.q: The limiting reactant determines:
quiz.explanation: Limiting reactant is completely consumed first, setting the theoretical maximum yield of products.

quiz.q: If theoretical yield is 10g and actual is 8g, percentage yield is:
For 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, 4 dm³ H₂ reacts with how much O₂?
quiz.explanation: Volume ratio H₂:O₂ = 2:1. So 4 dm³ H₂ requires 2 dm³ O₂.

quiz.q: Which reactant is in excess if 2 mol A reacts with 3 mol B in A + 2B → C?
quiz.explanation: 2 mol A requires 4 mol B. Only 3 mol B available, so B is limiting, A is in excess.

quiz.q: Mass of 2 mol of NaOH is: (Na=23, O=16, H=1)
Percentage yield is always less than 100% because:
opts: Side reactions, incomplete conversion, and experimental losses
quiz.explanation: Real reactions have practical losses: side products, equilibrium limits, transfer losses, incomplete reactions.

quiz.q: In mass-volume calculations at non-STP conditions, you should use:
quiz.explanation: PV=nRT accounts for actual T and P, unlike the fixed 22.4 dm³/mol which only applies at STP.

subUnits.title: 8.2 SI Units & Dimensional Analysis in Chemistry
notes: 1. Length: meter (m), common: cm, mm, nm
6. Volume: cubic meter (m³), common: dm³ (L), cm³ (mL)
• Always use conversion factors that cancel unwanted units
• Example: 500 cm³ to dm³: 500 cm³ × (1 dm³/1000 cm³) = 0.5 dm³
• Multiply given value by conversion factors arranged so units cancel
• Final unit must match required quantity
2. Check that final units match the question
3. Use scientific notation for very large/small numbers
4. Verify significant figures match given data
• Unit conversion questions frequently test cm³ ↔ dm³, °C ↔ K, g ↔ kg
• Concentration calculations require consistent volume units (usually dm³)
• Gas law problems require absolute temperature (Kelvin)
diagramDescriptions: SI unit chart for chemistry with base units, symbols, and common conversions
Dimensional analysis step-by-step flowchart showing unit cancellation
Volume conversion pyramid: m³ → dm³(L) → cm³(mL) → mm³(μL)
Temperature scale comparison: Celsius vs Kelvin with freezing/boiling points marked
subUnits.youtubeQuery: SI units dimensional analysis chemistry conversions WAEC site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The SI unit for amount of substance is:
quiz.explanation: Mole (mol) is the SI base unit for amount of substance.

quiz.q: To convert cm³ to dm³, you should:
quiz.explanation: 1 dm³ = 1000 cm³, so divide cm³ by 1000 to get dm³.

quiz.q: 25°C is equal to how many Kelvin?
Which unit is correct for molar concentration?
quiz.explanation: Molarity = moles of solute per volume of solution in dm³ (mol/dm³ or M).

quiz.q: 1 atm is equivalent to:
quiz.explanation: Standard pressure: 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 760 torr.

quiz.q: In dimensional analysis, units cancel when:
opts: Same unit appears in numerator and denominator
quiz.explanation: Conversion factors are arranged so unwanted units cancel (numerator/denominator), leaving desired unit.

quiz.q: 0.5 kg is equal to:
Why must temperature be in Kelvin for gas law calculations?
opts: Kelvin has no negative values, ensuring proportional relationships
quiz.explanation: Gas laws (Charles, Gay-Lussac, Ideal) rely on absolute temperature. 0 K = absolute zero, ensuring V ∝ T works mathematically.

quiz.q: Which conversion factor correctly converts mol to molecules?
quiz.explanation: Avogadro's constant: 6.022×10²³ particles per 1 mole.

quiz.q: A common mistake in concentration calculations is:
opts: Forgetting to convert volume to consistent units (usually dm³)
quiz.explanation: Molarity requires volume in dm³. Using cm³ without conversion gives answers 1000× too large.
